rulings.Case Title: MARTIFER-SILVERADO FUND I, LLC VS. TALESUN SOLAR USA LTD
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judicial Determination Of Martifer-Silverado Fund I, Llcs Attorney-Client Privilege Claim
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday September 5, 2019. Line 2 - DEFENDANTs TALESUN SOLAR USA LTD and ZHONGLI NEW ENERGY USA CO., LLC's Motion For Judicial Determination Of Martifer-Silverado Fund I, LLCs Attorney-Client Privilege Claim. Pro Tem Judge Julia Campins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to julia@campinsbenhambaker.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(JPT/302)

rulings.Case Title: STEPHANIE NEHMENS VS. FCA US, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Attorney Fees, Costs, And Expenses
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 5, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF STEPHANIE NEHMENS' Motion For Attorney Fees, Costs, And Expenses: Plaintiff Stephanie Nehmens's motion for attorney fees, costs, and expenses is granted in the amount of $50,817.92 in attorney's fees and $7,051.23 in costs and expenses. In defendant's December 11, 2018 Code of Civil Procedure section 998 offer, which plaintiff accepted on January 15, 2019, defendant offered "to pay reasonable costs, expenses and attorney's fees based on actual time expended pursuant to Civil Code section 1794(b) as stipulated by the parties or, if the parties cannot agree, upon motion to the Court having jurisdiction over this action." (Cutler Decl., Ex. 4.) Despite the emphasized language, plaintiff did not make any effort to stipulate to an award of reasonable attorneys' fees, costs and expenses until August 12, 2019, after counsel had already expended nearly $9,000 in attorney's fees in preparing the instant motion. (Id. 24; Shahian Decl., Ex. 31.) Further, a large number of attorneys (nine) billed time on the case on plaintiff's behalf, which necessarily resulted in substantial duplication and inefficiency. For these reasons, the Court finds that not all of the time for which plaintiff seeks compensation was necessary and reasonably incurred. (See Etcheson v. FCA US LLC (2018) 30 Cal.App.5th 831, 840 ["A prevailing buyer has the burden of 'showing that the fees incurred were "allowable," were "reasonably necessary to the conduct of the litigation," and were "reasonable in amount."'"].) In order to determine a reasonable award, the Court declines to award any compensation for time spent in connection with the fee motion, which could have been entirely avoided, and has reduced the remaining time by 25%, to $50,817.92, which the Court finds to be a reasonable lodestar. (See, e.g., Warren v. Kia Motors America, Inc. (2018) 30 Cal.App.5th 24, 37, 40-41 [trial court may apply a negative multiplier to adjust the lodestar figure to arrive at a reasonable fee if it "clearly explain[s] its case-specific reasons for the percentage reduction," which may properly include excessive time spent on case by multiple attorneys]; Save Our Uniquely Rural Community Environment v. County of San Bernardino (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1179, 1188 [trial court did not abuse its discretion in reducing lodestar by 80 percent].) The Court declines to award a positive multiplier, as plaintiff has not shown that this "lemon law" case presented novel or difficult questions of law or fact. (See Hall v. FCA US LLC (E.D. Cal. May 21, 2018) 2018 WL 2298431 at *8 [declining to award multiplier to plaintiff's counsel in Song-Beverly Act case where, among other things, case did not present novel or difficult questions of law or fact, there was no evidence that the nature of the litigation precluded other employment by the attorneys, and the contingent nature of the fee award was outweighed by the other factors].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EVELYN DAVIDSON VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 5, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication Hearing required. Counsel should be prepared at the hearing to address the following questions and authorities: 1. Does plaintiff's undisputed status as a temporary employee (UMF 3-6) preclude her claims? (See Jenkins v. County of Riverside (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 593, 616-617 [trial court properly granted summary judgment on FEHA claim by plaintiff, who worked more than the 1,040 hours allowed to a temporary employee]; see also Scotch v. Art Institute of California (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 986, 994 [plaintiff's proposed accommodation "amounted to a guarantee of full-time employment, which [defendant] was not required to provide"].) 2. Should the Court strike Paragraph 4 of plaintiff's declaration ( 4) in light of her deposition testimony? (See D'Amico v. Board of Supervisors (1974) 11 Cal.3d 1, 21-22.) 3. Was plaintiff's initial request for a new chair, coupled with her failure to follow up with her supervisor or the human resources department on that subject (UMF 23), sufficient to discharge her burden under the interactive process? (See Scotch v. Art Institute of California, 173 Cal.App.4th at 1014 ["Both employer and employee have the obligation 'to keep communications open' and neither has "a right to obstruct the process.' [Citation.] 'Each party must participate in good faith, undertake reasonable efforts to communicate its concerns, and make available to the other information which is available, or more accessible, to one party. Liability hinges on the objective circumstances surrounding the parties' breakdown in communication, and responsibility for the breakdown lies with the party who fails to participate in good faith.'"].) 4. It is undisputed that the three other Contract Compliance Officer I's initially hired with plaintiff in February 2016 applied for and were hired into permanent civil service positions. (Royer Decl. 4, Ex. 3 at 4.) Is there any evidence in the record that plaintiff applied for or was placed on the eligible list for such a position? If not, does the City's decision to hire those individuals as permanent civil service employees provide any basis for inferring that plaintiff was subjected to disparate treatment discrimination or that the City's reason for terminating her was pretextual? =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LUU TRAN ET AL VS. JAVELIN LOGISTICS COMPANY, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 5, 2019, Line 7, CROSS DEFENDANT MENZIES AVIATION (USA) INC.'s DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT Cross-defendant Menzies Aviation (USA) Inc.'s demurrer to the amended cross-complaint of the City and County of San Francisco and San Francisco International Airport based upon Code of Civil Procedure § 430.10(c) is overruled. Such a plea in abatement requires an "absolute identity of parties [and] causes of action." (Plant Insulation Co. v. Fibreboard Corp. (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 781, 788; Childs v. Eltinge (1973) 29 Cal.App.3d 843, 848 ["a demurrer may be sustained on the ground that another cause of action is pending only when the parties in the two cases are identical and the cause of action and issues in the two actions are substantially the same."].) Here, Menzies is one of two plaintiffs in the declaratory relief action (No. CGC-19-574401), together with its insurer Global Aerospace Underwriting Managers, which is not named as a party in the instant tort action. Moreover, Menzies concedes in its reply that "the declaratory judgment action and the underlying litigation raise separate questions." (Opposition, at 3.) The motion based upon exclusive concurrent jurisdiction is denied. The rule of exclusive concurrent jurisdiction does not apply because the actions are pending in the same court in San Francisco. "We held in Kromm v. Kromm, 84 Cal.App.2d 523 [191 P.2d 115], that there is no priority of jurisdiction between cases filed in the same court in the same county, nor is there any conflict of jurisdiction between them." (Daly v. White (1950) 100 Cal.App.2d 22, 25.) The propriety of staying the declaratory relief action pursuant to Montrose Chemical Corp. v. Superior Court (1993) 6 Cal.4th 287 is not presently before the court. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SUSAN BELL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses From Defendant
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 5, 2019, Line 9, PLAINTIFF SUSAN BELL's Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses From Defendant The report and recommendation of the judge pro tem is adopted. Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses to discovery responses from defendant is granted on the following conditions: (1) the Court will enter the parties' stipulated confidentiality order, which the parties shall submit at or prior to the hearing; (2) the City shall produce the body worn camera videos, without further redactions, and shall designate those portions of the videos that it contends are entitled to confidential treatment under applicable law; (3) the City shall provide a description of any complaints it received concerning police use of excessive force relating to the clearing operation on July 9, 2018, regardless of the extent of any injury allegedly suffered; (4) the City shall provide the number of complaints of use of excessive force, regardless of the extent of any injury allegedly suffered, made in the last 10 years against Officer Jensen, Officer Neary, and the "presently unidentified officer(s) [who] moved Plaintiff closer to" Officers Jensen and Neary, if and when identified; (5) the City shall provide discovery verifications in the form recommended by the judge pro tem. The parties' requests for discovery sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = (302/EPS)
